---
title: "Teaching tags"
subtitle: "Which tags are gendered, and how well tags predict rating & difficulty (Q4, Q8, Q9)"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| echo: false
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from IPython.display import display
from ape import questions


def show(figs, *keys):
    """Display only the named figures; close the rest so nothing leaks."""
    for k, f in figs.items():
        if k not in keys:
            plt.close(f)
    for k in keys:
        display(figs[k])
    plt.close("all")
```

## Q4 — Which tags differ by gender?

We test each of the 20 tags (normalized per rating) for a male/female difference, then control the
false-discovery rate across all 20 with **Benjamini–Hochberg** at α = 0.005.

```{python}
#| label: fig-q4
#| fig-cap: "Per-tag gender difference as −log₁₀(FDR-adjusted p). Bars past the red line are significant; grey bars are not."
res, figs = questions.q4()
print(f"{res['n_significant_fdr']} of 20 tags differ significantly by gender (FDR @ 0.005)")
print("most gendered :", ", ".join(res["most_gendered"]))
print("least gendered:", ", ".join(res["least_gendered"]))
show(figs, "q4_tag_significance")
```

**Finding.** **17 of 20** tags are gendered. *Hilarious* is overwhelmingly the most gendered (men get
it far more), followed by *Caring* and *Amazing lectures*. The least gendered are *Tough grader*,
*Accessible*, and *Pop quizzes!* — the only three that don't clear the bar.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Why FDR (and why this differs from the original)
With 20 tests, uncorrected p < 0.05 would expect ~1 false positive by chance; the original used that
threshold. **Benjamini–Hochberg** controls the expected *proportion* of false discoveries — less
conservative than Bonferroni, appropriate when we expect many true effects. With proper per-rating
normalization, *Pop quizzes!* (flagged in the original) falls to the **least** gendered tag.
:::

## Q8 — Predict average *rating* from tags

Forward selection (leak-free 5-fold CV) over the 20 tags; coefficients are standardized for
comparability.

```{python}
#| label: fig-q8
#| fig-cap: "Standardized coefficients of the selected tag model for average rating."
res8, figs8 = questions.q8()
print("selected tags:", ", ".join(res8["best_features"]))
print(f"R² = {res8['best_r2']:.3f}   RMSE = {res8['best_rmse']:.3f}")
print(f"strongest predictor: {res8['top_predictor']} (β = {res8['top_predictor_beta']:.3f})")
show(figs8, "q8_coefficients")
```

**Finding.** Tags explain a lot of rating variance (**R² ≈ 0.74**). **Tough grader** is the strongest
predictor and it's *negative* — being seen as a tough grader pulls ratings down. This is a touch
weaker than the numeric-feature model in [Models](models.qmd), where "would retake" alone is
extremely predictive.

## Q9 — Predict average *difficulty* from tags

```{python}
#| label: fig-q9
#| fig-cap: "Standardized coefficients of the selected tag model for average difficulty."
res9, figs9 = questions.q9()
print("selected tags:", ", ".join(res9["best_features"]))
print(f"R² = {res9['best_r2']:.3f}   RMSE = {res9['best_rmse']:.3f}")
print(f"strongest predictor: {res9['top_predictor']} (β = {res9['top_predictor_beta']:.3f})")
show(figs9, "q9_coefficients")
```

**Finding.** Tags predict difficulty less well than rating (**R² ≈ 0.60**). Here **Tough grader** is
the strongest predictor and *positive* — the mirror image of Q8, which makes intuitive sense: tough
graders are rated harder and liked less.
